zKiBbpY_rVo.title: Chichewa 101.02 - learning to speak Chichewa
zKiBbpY_rVo.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa language lesson. The instructor speaks English and teaches Chichewa (Chinyanja) words and phrases. Common Chichewa words: moni (hello), zikomo (thank you), inde (yes), ayi (no), bwa...

Kq-cdTrurJ0.title: Chichewa 101.03 - learning to speak Chichewa
Kq-cdTrurJ0.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa language lesson. The instructor speaks English and teaches Chichewa (Chinyanja) words and phrases. Common Chichewa words: moni (hello), zikomo (thank you), inde (yes), ayi (no), bwa...

segments.text: Lesson number 3. Today is the beginning of verbs. The verb to eat is kudya. I am eating is ndikudya.
You don't have to sound like a girl.
Yeah, that is correct. I don't know why I look at my notes. You, plural, are eating is mkudya. They are eating is akudya. So the same as he, she and they, it's all the same.
Future tense is quite simple as well. You just remove the ku and what we just declined and say, so I will eat is ndidya.
You will eat is udya. He or she will eat is adya. We will eat is didya. They will eat is adya.
Depending on how you say it, it can be a question. So, didya is I will eat and didya is will I eat. Similarly, you will eat is udya but to say will you eat is udya.
So you can ask a kid, udya, will you eat? And he or she or they will eat is adya. Well done!
What about you, plural? You, plural, will eat is didya. No, mkudya. And will they eat is, sorry, will we eat is didya.
So, as a statement, that we will eat is didya and will you eat is didya. You're asking yourself, didya.
And that is it. You've learned the present tense and the future tense. End of lesson 3.
Of one verb. Of one verb. You'll learn many verbs in the next few weeks.

uNE_znhZPdg.title: Chichewa 101.06 - learning to speak Chichewa
uNE_znhZPdg.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa language lesson. The instructor speaks English and teaches Chichewa (Chinyanja) words and phrases. Common Chichewa words: moni (hello), zikomo (thank you), inde (yes), ayi (no), bwa...

segments.text: Lesson 6. We're going to learn the past tense, so simple past like I ate, and the past tense, progressive, I was eating.
To create a past tense, you take a verb like kuya, which is to eat, remove the ku, and you add a prefix, depending on whether it's I, he, she, it, and so forth.
I ate is ndinadja, you ate is unadja, you ate plural is munadja, he, she, or they ate is anadja, we ate is dinadja.
Perfect. So, whereas before you had ndija, now you've got an na. There's an na between all of those pronouns, personal pronouns.
To make it I was eating, you put an ma. So, ndimadja is I was eating.
You were eating is umadja. Mumadja is you were eating plural. He or she was eating is amadja. They were eating is also amadja.
We were eating is timadja. I've got a little trick for differentiating between ndima and dina. So, ndimadja is I was eating, and you can take the m, flip it around, and it's a w for was.
And that helps you remember it. As opposed to ndinadja, I ate, ndimadja, I was eating, just flip the m around. I hope that helps you the same way it will help her. Thank you.

As opposed to ndinadja, I ate, ndimadja, I was eating, just flip the m around. I hope that helps you the same way it will help her. Thank you. Zigoomo.

NKBih9TSw74.title: Chichewa 101.07 - learning to speak Chichewa
NKBih9TSw74.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa language lesson. The instructor speaks English and teaches Chichewa (Chinyanja) words and phrases. Common Chichewa words: moni (hello), zikomo (thank you), inde (yes), ayi (no), bwa...

segments.text: Lesson 7. A few more verbs. To run is kutamanga. To lie is gunama. And this is lying like being deceitful. To complain is kudandaula.
The first D was too strong. Kudandaula. To hide is kubisa. And that's when you hide something. Kubisa. And to hide yourself is kubisala. So I am hiding is ndikubisala.
But if you want to say, I am hiding this thing here, ndikubisa. Exactly. To jump is kulumpa or kudumpa. Kulumpa, kudumpa. To walk, kuyenda. To read, kuelenga. To run, kutamanga. End of lesson 7.

EKuBXtEUHoo.title: Chichewa 101.09 - learning to speak Chichewa
EKuBXtEUHoo.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa language lesson. The instructor speaks English and teaches Chichewa (Chinyanja) words and phrases. Common Chichewa words: moni (hello), zikomo (thank you), inde (yes), ayi (no), bwa...

segments.text: Lesson 9. A few final verbs and commands. To make is kubanga. To help is kutandiza. To swear, as in cuss, is kutukwana. To leave something is kusia.
To go is kupita. To come is kwera. So, I'm coming is dikwera. Nao means also. So, also is nao. So, if you want to say, I will also come, you will say dikwera nao.
Now, if you want to command someone to do something, there are two ways to do it. Either, just remove the ku bit of the verb. So, to go is kupita. You can say pita. It just means go.

Or, you can remove the ku and add ta. And, it sort of means, you should go. So, tapita is you should go. So, command, either just remove the ku and then continue the verb. Or, add ta at the beginning of the verb and remove the ku.

As I think about it, however, kuya is to eat. If you wanted to say eat, you can't just say ya. You have to say iya. So, I think there are special circumstances. And, as you go along speaking with someone who is teaching you Jijewa, they will tell you these special circumstances.
The more common command is to add ta. Remove ku and add ta. So, ta ya is eat. Thank you. End of lesson 9.

EKuBXtEUHoo.full_text: Lesson 9. A few final verbs and commands. To make is kubanga. To help is kutandiza. To swear, as in cuss, is kutukwana. To leave something is kusia. To go is kupita. To come is kwera. So, I'm coming is dikwera. Nao means also. So, also is nao. So, if you want to say, I will also come, you will say dikwera nao. Now, if you want to command someone to do something, there are two ways to do it. Either, just remove the ku bit of the verb. So, to go is kupita. You can say pita. It just means go. Or, you can remove the ku and add ta. And, it sort of means, you should go. So, tapita is you should go. So, command, either just remove the ku and then continue the verb. Or, add ta at the beginning of the verb and remove the ku. As I think about it, however, kuya is to eat. If you wanted to say eat, you can't just say ya. You have to say iya. So, I think there are special circumstances. And, as you go along speaking with someone who is teaching you Jijewa, they will tell you these special circumstances. The more common command is to add ta. Remove ku and add ta. So, ta ya is eat. Thank you. End of lesson 9.

Q6H38TwYA6o.title: Chichewa 101.10 - learning to speak Chichewa
Q6H38TwYA6o.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa language lesson. The instructor speaks English and teaches Chichewa (Chinyanja) words and phrases. Common Chichewa words: moni (hello), zikomo (thank you), inde (yes), ayi (no), bwa...

segments.text: Lesson number 10. Who is Ndani? Ndani. What is Jani? Jani. Where is Kuti? Kuti. When is Liti? Liti. How is Bwanji? Bwanji.

Two more things for today's lesson. If you want to say, I will not do something, either not bit, you just add si in front of the personal pronouns. So, ndikufuna means I want, si ndikufuna is I don't want.
Or, you are going is uguvita and you will not go is suguvita. The si and the u are said so quickly that the i is almost absent. Suguvita means you will not go, you are not going.
Finally, a special verb, to have. You just add li in front of, after the personal pronouns, not in front of. Ndili means I have. Uli means you have. He, she or they have is ali.
We have is dili and you plural have is muli. Typically, this have is followed by ndi. So, ndili ndi wuku is I have a book. End of lesson 10.

If you want to say, I will not do something, either not bit, you just add si in front of the personal pronouns. So, ndikufuna means I want, si ndikufuna is I don't want. Or, you are going is uguvita and you will not go is suguvita. The si and the u are said so quickly that the i is almost absent. Suguvita means you will not go, you are not going. Finally, a special verb, to have. You just add li in front of, after the personal pronouns, not in front of. Ndili means I have. Uli means you have. He, she or they have is ali. We have is dili and you plural have is muli. Typically, this have is followed by ndi. So, ndili ndi wuku is I have a book. End of lesson 10.

0l6hR_7xFyo.title: Chichewa 101.11 - learning to speak Chichewa
0l6hR_7xFyo.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa language lesson. The instructor speaks English and teaches Chichewa (Chinyanja) words and phrases. Common Chichewa words: moni (hello), zikomo (thank you), inde (yes), ayi (no), bwa...

segments.text: Lesson number 11. A person is munthu. Munthu. People is anthu. Anthu. Father is bambo. Bambo.
And if I want to say my father, I'll say bamboanga. Bamboanga. Mother is mayi. Mayi. Similarly, my mother is mayianga. Mayianga.
Child is mwana. Mwana. Boy is nyamada. Nyamada. Girl is ntsikana. Ntsikana. God is mulungu. Mulungu.
And sometimes he will use the word jawuta, which I think means almighty. Jawuta. Perfect. End of lesson 11. Zikomo.

Smjyi0PnbWo.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa language lesson. The instructor speaks English and teaches Chichewa (Chinyanja) words and phrases. Common Chichewa words: moni (hello), zikomo (thank you), inde (yes), ayi (no), bwa...

segments.text: Harry and I are in Neno village in Mwanza in Malawi today and we have a few
interesting words. Children is ana. Ana. One child is mwana. Mwana. And that's my little nephew Christopher. Good boy.
Rock is mwala. Mwala. Sand, this type of sand is doti. Doti. And you have to differentiate that from beach sand which is njenga. Njenga.
Thatched roof is denga lama uzu. Denga lama uzu.
Mats, the village mats, the woven mats, are called ngeka. Ngeka.
And finally, papaya is papaya. Papaya. Perfect.

Thatched roof is denga lama uzu. Denga lama uzu. Mats, the village mats, the woven mats, are called ngeka. Ngeka. And finally, papaya is papaya. Papaya. Perfect.
